attributes.stem:
A 59-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with urinary retention two weeks after surgical resection of a spinal tumour at T6 level. She was initially making good progress on a rehabilitation ward but was discharged home at her request. She has a medical history of hypertension and takes amlodipine. Prior to her diagnosis, she lived alone, worked as an accountant, and was fully independent with activities of daily living.

On examination, she appears stable. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She has grade 3/5 power in both lower limbs with increased tone, reduced sensation below T6, and hyperreflexia. She requires assistance for transfers and has an indwelling urinary catheter placed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Trial of void with bladder scanner
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with neurogenic bladder dysfunction following incomplete spinal cord injury. The development of urinary retention requires careful assessment and management to prevent complications such as urinary tract infections and upper urinary tract damage.

Urological referral is the most appropriate next step as specialist assessment is needed to determine the type of neurogenic bladder dysfunction (e.g., detrusor overactivity, detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia) through urodynamic studies. This will guide specific management strategies and long term planning.

Clean intermittent catheterisation may ultimately be part of the management plan, but should only be implemented after proper urological assessment and training in a rehabilitation setting.

Indwelling suprapubic catheter is a more invasive option for long term management of bladder dysfunction that would only be considered if other methods fail or are unsuitable. It carries risks of infection and should not be first-line management. It is preferable to long term indwelling catheters for most patients.

Trial of void with bladder scanner would be inappropriate at this stage as the patient has neurogenic bladder dysfunction that requires specialist assessment first.

Anticholinergic medication may be useful for managing detrusor overactivity, but should only be prescribed after proper urological assessment and urodynamic studies.

The management of neurogenic bladder requires a comprehensive approach including prevention of complications, maintaining continence, and protecting upper urinary tract function. This is best achieved through specialist urological assessment followed by implementation of appropriate bladder management strategies in a rehabilitation setting.
learningPoints: Urodynamic studies are essential to determine the type of neurogenic bladder dysfunction and guide management in spinal cord injury.
Early discharge from rehabilitation after spinal cord injury increases risk of complications like urinary retention.
Management of neurogenic bladder requires specialist assessment before implementing specific continence strategies.
Clean intermittent catheterisation should only be started after proper urological assessment and patient training.
references: NSW Agency for Clinical Innovation. Spinal cord injury bladder management a guide for clinicians in non-specialist units. Sydney ACI ; 2023. Available online at ; https://aci.health.nsw.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/891591/ACI-spinal-cord-injury-bladder-management-a-guide-for-clinicians-in-non-specialist-units.pdf

Sekido N, Igawa Y, Kakizaki H, Kitta T, Sengoku A, Takahashi S, Takahashi R, Tanaka K, Namima T, Honda M, Mitsui T. Clinical guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of lower urinary tract dysfunction in patients with spinal cord injury. International Journal of Urology. 2020 Apr;27(4):276-88. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/iju.14186
